meta.title: Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE LOCAL EXPERIENCE Yamanaka Onsen, Kaga, Ishikawa, Japan Yamanaka Onsen, Kaga, Ishikawa, Japan The Mountains that live forever and gentle valleys. Purple clouds and murmuring streams, tinted by the changing light. In the midst of nature that produces unparalleled scenery, we convey the comfort that has been passed down from generation to generation, as well as new culture. LOCATION Hanamurasaki is a traditional Japanese inn but it has unique style. Hanamurasaki at Yamanaka Onsen is in a location which is almost the same distance from major cities such as Tokyo, Osaka, Kyoto, and Nagoya, moreover is very easily accessible and just about 30 min by car(taxi) from Komatsu Airport. CUISINE We serve dishes imbued with the climate of Ishikawa. Our chic seasonal menu is available as a kaiseki course meal, or a la carte so guests can choose according to their tastes and dietary restrictions. We offer beverage pairings including sake, wine, beer, and tea. This dining experience is rooted in the traditions and philosophy of Japanese cuisine, while incorporating contemporary creativity. MORE GUEST ROOMS From every room, you can see Kakusenkei gorge. The grandeur of nature and a sense of serenity that can only be found in Yamanaka Onsen flows through the guest rooms. We encourage guests to relax their bodies and minds in the healing space, where ancient elegance and modern comfort are woven together. MORE SABO Our sabo, or tea room, is a special place where guests can peacefully commune with themselves and others. We offer a selection of teas, including sencha, Japanese black tea, Kaga bōcha (a local variety of roasted twig tea), and even tea cocktails, all served using one-of-a-kind tea utensils created in collaboration with artists. We hope to create a moment of connection through our unique modern style of tea experience. MORE ONSEN Yamanaka onsen is natural hot spring from boasting more than 1300 years history. Feel healing by the high quality hot spring. The onsen is not only great relaxation but also good for health and skin, giving the skin a silky and smooth texture. LOCAL EXPERIENCE At Hanamurasaki, we offer exclusive tours that are deeply rooted in the local culture. Immerse yourself in the natural beauty, traditional crafts, and unique art that this land has to offer. MORE OUR STORY Hanamurasaki traces its roots back to 1902, when its predecessor, Yamadaya, first opened its doors. At the time, inns with natural hot springs were few and far between, and Yamadaya soon became a cherished retreat for travellers. In 1984, inspired by the gentle beauty of the surrounding mountain valley, the owner of the previous generation established Hanamurasaki. Its name comes from the poetic phrase Sanshi Suimei – “purple mountains and clear waters” – an expression that captures the fleeting, one-of-a-kind beauty found in nature. Today, Hanamurasaki carries this spirit forward. We continue to explore what it means to share Japanese culture in a contemporary context, with the hope of quietly passing it on to those who come next. MORE ACCESS Address 1-Ho17-1, Higashimachi Yamanaka Onsen, Kaga, Ishikawa, Japan 922-0114 Tel +81-761-78-0077 Parking Our staff will guide you from the ryokan entrance to the parking area. There is no charge for guest parking. Check-in 14:00 (*Last check-in at 18:00) Check-out 11:00 To Google Map About check-in About guest room About meals About facilities About reservation About the season Q What are your check-in and check-out times? A Check-in is from 2:00pm. and check-out is at 11:00am. Q How late can I check in? What should I do if I will be checking in late? A Please note that if we do not hear from you, we may consider your reservation cancelled as of 6:00pm of the date reserved. Q Can you hold my luggage before I check in or after I check out? A We'll be pleased to take care of your luggage at the front desk. Q Is there free parking lot for guests? A Yes, there is. Please park in front of the entrance. Our staff will move your car to the parking lot. Q Is there a free shuttle for guests? A We offer free shuttle service from Kagaonsen station between 12pm and 6pm, by reservation. If you would like to take the shuttle, please let us know via email your arrival time and train name at Kagaonsen station one day in advance of your stay. The return shuttle to Kagaonsen is available from 9:00am to 11:30am. Q Is there free shuttle from Komatsu Airport? A Unfortunately, no, there isn’t. Free shuttle is from Kagaonsen station only. Please take a taxi from Komatsu Airport. Q Are there any open-air bath with rooms? A Yes, there are 3 comfort suite rooms and 2 Jr.suite rooms installed open-air bath. Q Are there non-smoking rooms? A Yes, all rooms are non-smoking rooms. Q Are there bed rooms? A Yes, Comfort suite rooms and Jr.suite rooms are bed rooms. Other room types are Japanese futon bedding but we could provide rollaway bed on request as well. Q Is there a nicer view room? A All rooms are facing the river and have nice view. Q I have a food allergy. A It corresponds to the extent possible If you will tell us in advance via email. Q When should I choose a la carte kaiseki menu? A A la carte menu is in your room. Please choose by dinner. Q Is it possible to serve a la carte kaiseki in room dining? A It is not possible. A la carte kaiseki can be served dining restaurant only. Q Is it possible to have meals with under 10years old child at the dining restaurant. A It is not possible to enter with under 10yeards old child to the dining restaurant. If you stay with under 10years old child, please choose in-room dining plan. Q What time do you serve dinner and breakfast? A It starts serving from 6pm to 7pm for dinner, and 7:30am to 9:00am for breakfast. Q Is free Wi-Fi internet access available? A We have free Wi-Fi in the room and the lobby. Q Which credit cards do you accept? A We accept the following cards: VISA, JCB, AMEX, Diners, UC, DC, NICOS, Master Card, AEON, LIFE, ORICO, 銀聯. Q Can I request a massage in my room? A Massages are available in your room. The massages are sometimes crowded, so it's better to make a reservation before your stay via email. Q Can I send a package from Hanamurasaki to another hotel? A We accept only domestic shipping. We do not accept international shipping from here. Q Is it wheelchair accessible? A It is accessible in the facility and we provide complimentary usage of wheelchairs within the ryokan. There are 2 accessible Japanese western comfort suite rooms with wheelchair accessible a private hot spring bath. It is not accessible for traditional Japanese rooms. Q Can I bring some drinks during dinner and breakfast? A We do not accept drink from outside during dinner and breakfast. It is fine to have drinks in your room. Q Do you provide pajamas? A All rooms are provided with traditional yukata robes as pajamas. Q How long does it take to Yuge kaido(main street of Yamanaka town) by walk? Can you take me there by car? A It takes about 15min by walk. We do not offer taking a guest there. We offer free shuttle from Kaganonsen station only. Q Do you offer a day trip bathing. A We do not offer a day trip bathing. It is for staying guests only. Q Do you open for lunch? A We offer Japanese style Afternoon tea for lunch reservation needed. Q Can I make a reservation on the day I wish to arrive? A Yes, please make a reservation through our official web site. Q What are your cancellation charges? A The following charges will apply if you cancel your reservations. Cancellation 14days to 6days before arrival: 30% Cancellation 5day before arrival: 50% Cancellation 3days to on the day of arrival: 100% No-show: 100% Click here for cancellation policy Q What season is usually best to see the cherry blossoms and the autumn leaves? A It is usually best in the beginning of April for the cherry blossoms, and best in the middle to the end of November. Q Is it necessary driving with snow tire in winter season? A It is necessary during winter. It is usually snow tire installed for a rental car in winter.

meta.title: CUISINE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: We offer a variety of meals that reflect the changing seasons and the spirit of Ishikawa Prefecture, thoughtfully prepared with carefully selected seasonal ingredients and artful tableware. In addition to authentic kaiseki cuisine, we also provide menu options catering to guests' dietary requirements and restrictions, along with tastefully curated pairings of sake and other beverages. Rooted in the traditions and philosophy of Japanese cuisine, each dining experience is elevated with a touch of contemporary creativity inviting you to discover a new expression of Japanese cuisine. Our head chef creates each menu with care, using the best seasonal ingredients. From appetisers to desserts, you can enjoy Japanese traditions alongside the fresh flavours of the local area. An Example of Our Menu Appetizer Seasonal Selection of the Day Soup Scallop and Yuba Fishcake Dumpling Sashimi Assorted Seasonal Sashimi Simmered Dish Steamed Tilefish with Lotus Root Grilled Dish Grilled Mackerel Marinated in Miso and Yuzu Vinegared Dish Snow Crab and Whelk with Achara Vinegar Dressing Main Course Roasted Noto Beef Dessert Noto Salt Ice Cream *The menu changes monthly. *Vegetarian Kaiseki is here . Our head chef creates each menu with care, using the best seasonal ingredients. From appetisers to desserts, you can enjoy Japanese traditions alongside the fresh flavours of the local area. An Example of Our Menu Appetizer Seasonal Selection of the Day Soup Scallop and Yuba Fishcake Dumpling Sashimi Assorted Seasonal Sashimi Simmered Dish Steamed Tilefish with Lotus Root Grilled Dish Grilled Spanish Mackerel Marinated in Miso and Yuzu Vinegared Dish Snow Crab and Whelk with Achara Vinegar Dressing Main Course Roasted Noto Beef Dessert Noto Salt Ice Cream *The menu changes monthly. *Vegetarian Kaiseki is here . In winter, we welcome the arrival of Kanō crab, a celebrated delicacy of Ishikawa and a much-loved local variety of snow crab. Each guest is served the equivalent of one whole, fresh crab, carefully prepared using live crabs caught at the nearby fishing port, accompanied by a selection of seasonal dishes. It’s a special taste of the region we hope you’ll enjoy. This plan is available from 7th November to 20th March. Please note that due to adverse weather, the period may be subject to change, or crabs from other parts of the Sea of Japan may be used. Thank you for your understanding. Our menus highlight carefully selected seasonal vegetables and traditional local ingredients, such as Kaga vegetables, offering dishes that are also suitable for vegetarian guests. At Hanamurasaki, we do our utmost to accommodate guests with food allergies. While we are not able to completely guarantee allergen-free meals, we will take care to prepare dishes without the specified ingredients wherever possible. DETAIL We do our best to accommodate food allergies and dietary needs. However, please understand that making significant adjustments to the menu may incur an additional charge, and depending on the ingredients omitted, the number of dishes may be reduced. Japanese cuisine often relies on a variety of essential seasonings that can be difficult to remove completely. We appreciate your understanding in this regard. All meals are prepared in the same kitchen using shared equipment, including dishwashers and utensils. While we take great care, trace amounts of allergens may remain due to cross-contact during the cooking process. Because of this, we are unfortunately unable to guarantee a completely allergen-free environment. For guests with severe allergies, we may not be able to accommodate certain requests out of concern for your safety. We are happy to accommodate vegetarian, vegan, pescatarian, gluten-free, and other dietary preferences whenever possible. As same-day requests can be difficult to fulfil, we kindly ask that any dietary needs be shared with us when you book your stay. Please take a moment to review the above. If you have specific dietary concerns and would like to proceed, feel free to contact us by phone or include the details in the comment section of your reservation. We’ll be glad to assist you.

meta.title: GUEST ROOMS ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: From every room, you can see Kakusenkei gorge. The grandeur of nature and a sense of serenity that can only be found in Yamanaka Onsen flows through the guest rooms. We encourage guests to relax their bodies and minds in the healing space, where ancient elegance and modern comfort are woven together. A generous suite that feels almost like a public space, yet it remains yours alone. The open-air bath and sauna are complemented by original artwork, created in collaboration with local artists. This private retreat invites you to unwind in a moment where art and wellbeing gently come together. RESERVE A modern suite that quietly reimagines the enduring beauty of traditional Japanese architecture. A calm and inviting space shaped by the play of light and shadow, featuring a sunken kotatsu table crafted by a lacquer artist. With a private onsen and sauna, this suite offers a setting for slow, restorative time. RESERVE Nearly twice the size of a standard room, this suite includes a private open-air bath and sauna. Take in views of the Yamanaka Onsen valley as you relax in a space where comfort and openness come together. RESERVE Spanning a generous 120 square meters, this authentic Japanese room beautifully captures the essence of traditional architecture and serene living. The main room features tatami flooring, offering a peaceful retreat where you can fully relax. The adjoining bedroom is furnished with comfortable Western-style beds, providing an ideal blend of Japanese tradition and modern comfort. RESERVE The essence of Japanese architectural beauty is captured in this spacious and tranquil room. Featuring traditional tatami flooring and authentic futon bedding, it offers a serene setting where you can fully rest and refresh yourself. Enjoy the landscape and its seasonal changes, framed by the stunning views of Kakusenkei Gorge. RESERVE

meta.title: SABO ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: Our sabo, or tea room, is a special space for quiet reflection that invites you to connect with yourself and others through tea. We serve sencha, Kaga regional roasted tea, Japanese black tea, and original tea-based cocktails (chashu), all presented in thoughtfully crafted teaware created in collaboration with local artisans. Experience the spirit of chanoyu, Japan’s centuries-old tradition of hospitality, reimagined in a refined and contemporary style. At sabo, we offer a diverse menu starting with tea, alongside a selection of alcoholic beverages and sweets. Open to both ryokan guests and the general public. Plum Wine with Japanese Black Tea Sencha Gin & Tonic Matcha Beer Original Blended Tea Roasted Kaga Bōcha (Roasted Twig Tea) Local Ishikawa Black Tea Local Ishikawa Sencha Seasonal Fresh Tea Blend etc... RESERVE Enjoy a serene afternoon at Hanamurasaki with our exclusive Japanese-style afternoon tea.This thoughtfully curated experience features delicate vegetable sushi, seasonal wagashi (Japanese sweets), and a selection of premium Japanese teas. Each element is crafted to highlight the beauty of Japanese culinary tradition while offering a moment of pure relaxation. RESERVE

meta.title: LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: The concept of Hanamurasaki is to become a cultural salon that revitalizes traditions passed down through the ages. At Hanamurasaki, in our pursuit of becoming a true cultural salon in the modern era, we have launched an art tourism initiative. Visitors can immerse themselves in and freely engage in conversations about the culture of Yamanaka Onsen and Ishikawa, encompassing nature, culture, arts, and crafts. We believe it is our role to create as many opportunities like this as possible, continuously generating small but meaningful “ripples” of change. We also hope that the cultural ripples will continue to spread and overlap more than ever before. OWNER’S TOUR A Journey of Craft and Art with the Owner of Hanamurasaki. Time : Half day Price : ¥50,000 / person (transport and meals extra) Capacity : Up to 3 people (for guests staying 2 nights or more) To start planning your exclusive tour, please share your interests, and we’ll arrange a personalized experience. Please note that availability is limited, so we recommend booking in advance. MORE OWNER’S TOUR YAMANAKA ONSEN RETREAT Foodways and Craftways by Mokkei & Hanamurasaki Each retreat welcomes just 4–8 guests for deep cultural immersion and exchange, as well as relaxation. You’ll stay at Hanamurasaki for three nights, with daytime explorations rooted in the open air garden kitchen of a 100-year-old kominka (folk house) at Mokkei, and branching out around Yamanaka. Prepare and share meals together with local experts, using ingredients gathered and grown nearby. Visit the workshops of a craftsperson skilled in Yamanaka’s unique lacquerware. And immerse yourself in the wild and lush natural landscape. These moments invite you to reconnect with the land, with others, and with yourself. This isn’t a trip where you check things off a list, but rather a gentle opportunity to rediscover the textures of daily life. Rooted in the richness of Japan’s regional culture, this retreat proposes a new kind of travel that nurtures body and spirit through depth, not extravagance. meta.title: OUR STORY ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: I was born into a family that runs a ryokan (a traditional Japanese inn) and grew up with Japanese culture at the heart of my daily life. I must have soaked in the hot springs of Yamanaka hundreds, if not thousands, of times. Still, as a child, I found myself drawn to the world beyond Japan, always dreaming of what lay beyond these mountains. It wasn’t until after I spent time studying in the United States and graduated from university that I truly came to see the depth and beauty of what I had left behind. When I returned to Yamanaka Onsen after the early passing of my father, I saw my hometown and the culture I’d grown up with in a completely new light. Running a ryokan is, in many ways, like weaving together the threads of Japanese culture and lifestyle. In the course of just one night with two meals, guests can experience so many layers of what makes everyday life in Japan so special. I believe ryokans have an important role to play in not only sharing Japanese food, craft, philosophy, and design with the world, but also in passing these traditions on to the next generation. As the sixth-generation owner of Hanamurasaki, I see myself as continuing a path shaped by those before me, while also opening it up to new expressions. Rather than simply preserving the past, I hope to reflect its spirit in ways that speak to our time: creative, thoughtful, and open. Through your stay with us, I hope you will sense the care we put into sharing these values. Kohei Yamada Sixth-generation owner of Hanamurasaki Our ryokan began as Yamadaya, founded here in Yamanaka Onsen in 1902. Even back then, this town was known for its sō-yu, a shared public bath that drew visitors from across Japan, creating a place where inns gathered and stories were exchanged. There’s a phrase in Japanese onsen culture, hadaka no tsukiai, that speaks to the unguarded bonds formed by bathing together. It’s a reminder that hot springs are not just places to relax, but spaces where differences fade and connection grows. Over the years, this spirit helped shape the unique culture of hot spring towns, a heritage that lives on in Yamanaka today through folk songs, dances, and craft traditions passed down through generations. While embracing this deep-rooted onsen culture, Yamadaya stood out early on for something new: offering an indoor bath inside the inn itself – a rarity at the time. That quiet spirit of innovation is still part of who we are today. At Hanamurasaki, we honor tradition not by preserving it exactly as it was, but by reimagining it through a fresh, thoughtful lens keeping its essence alive while allowing it to evolve. The name Hanamurasaki was inspired by the phrase sanshi suimei, coined by the Edo-period thinker Rai Sanyō. It evokes a timeless landscape: purple-tinged mountains, clear streams, valleys wrapped in quiet beauty. It reminds us of nature’s ever-changing form, and of how no moment is ever the same. From every room at Hanamurasaki, you can gaze out at the seasonal beauty of Yamanaka Onsen’s valleys. Like the view, each guest experience is unique; shaped by the moment, the season, and the people sharing it. To spend time here is to step away from the everyday and into something more – a space for connection, reflection, and the gentle joys of travel. The name Hanamurasaki speaks to those fleeting, beautiful moments we share with our guests. In 2022, we began the renewal of the ryokan passed down to us by our ancestors. But rather than simply renovate, our hope was to express the essence of Japanese architecture, craftsmanship, cuisine, and tea culture in ways that speak to today, rooted in deep respect for the traditions that shaped them. Many of the artisans we call Living National Treasures were once bold innovators of their time. What we call tradition is not something fixed. It’s built on ideas that were once new, on creativity that was brave enough to endure. We believe that true craftsmanship grows from honoring what came before while continuing to explore what’s next. At first glance, inheritance and innovation may seem like opposites, but we believe they are two parts of the same story. The ryokan is a uniquely Japanese expression of hospitality. Within just one night, through two shared meals, guests are invited to experience the gentle rhythms of Japanese daily life. As part of our renewal, we created a tearoom within the ryokan, a small but meaningful space. In Japan, tea has long held a special place, connecting people and celebrating the everyday. We hope this room offers a moment to reconnect with something quietly enduring, even for those who may have forgotten its place in their own lives. Ryokans are often called “regional showrooms,” and we feel deeply connected to the community around us. Hot spring towns like Yamanaka have long drawn poets, potters, calligraphers, and other artists who found inspiration in the healing waters and left behind works that still speak today. That spirit of creativity is something we want to nurture, too. We collaborate with contemporary craftspeople and artists, sharing their work with guests while supporting their journeys in return. Just as ryokans once served as creative retreats, we hope Hanamurasaki can be a meeting place for today’s artists, a place where something meaningful can begin. To us, this is the true charm of a ryokan: not just a place to stay, but a cultural salon. If we could leave behind just one thing for the next hundred years, we would hope it to be the spirit of Japanese culture itself: a quiet appreciation for nature and the passing of time, a heart that finds richness in the present moment, a sense of beauty not in extravagance but in simplicity, and gratitude for each encounter. The skills of the craftspeople who shaped this world, and the values that guided them, may take different forms as time goes on, but they are part of something deeply human, and deeply Japanese. We hope that guests who visit Hanamurasaki, no matter where they’re from, will feel something of this spirit, and perhaps carry a part of it with them. And just maybe, they’ll add their own story to the thread that continues into the next hundred years. Sixth-generation owner of Hanamurasaki. After joining Yamadaya Co., Ltd. in 2011, he took on roles in corporate planning and executive management, and became President and CEO in 2021. A long-time admirer of street art, he studied art and photography at the Academy of Art University in San Francisco. After returning to Japan, he began bringing his creative sensibilities into the family business, launching a renewal project for Hanamurasaki in 2022. Today, Hanamurasaki showcases a growing collection of contemporary art and craft, a gallery space supporting local artists, and a tea room that invites guests to experience Japanese culture in a deeper, more immersive way. Under his supervision, the ryokan continues to grow as a space and community shaped by the spirit of a modern cultural salon of Japan. Inspired by the unique character of Yamanaka Onsen, a place where nature, daily life, and craftsmanship are closely intertwined, he remains committed to sharing its richness with guests, offering experiences that feel truly one of a kind.

meta.title: Owner’s Tour ｜ LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE TOUR PAGE A Journey of Craft and Art with the Owner of Hanamurasaki. Time : Half day Price : ¥50,000 / person (transport and meals extra) Capacity : Up to 3 people (for guests staying 2 nights or more) Explore the hidden gems of Yamanaka Onsen and immerse yourself in Japanese aesthetics with Kohei Yamada, the sixth-generation owner of Hanamurasaki. From private visits to craftsman studios to discovering secluded scenic spots, Kohei, who developed a deep appreciation for Japanese culture after studying abroad, offers insider access to this unique community, where he has built strong connections with local farmers and artisans. This personalized tour highlights the beauty and craftsmanship of Yamanaka Onsen, reflected in the exceptional service and philosophy of Hanamurasaki. Tour Sample #1 SATOYAMA Visit a traditional rural landscape with just one resident and enjoy Japanese architecture and hiking on this unique tour. This is the most natural area in Yamanaka Onsen. Recognized as an Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District, this region still retains the nostalgic landscapes that allow you to feel the traditional Japanese way of life passed down through generations. This natural area features flowing spring water, which is the purest source in Yamanaka Onsen. You can see Japanese plants and rice paddies at your feet. The tea experience, brewed with the town's abundant water, promises to be an exceptional moment. Tour Sample #2 LOCAL ART PLACE A tour visiting the studios of local artists and craftsmen, featuring two to three locations. This area is where many young and talented artists live. By visiting their studios and hearing directly about the themes and inspirations behind their work, you'll gain a deeper appreciation for their creations. In Ishikawa Prefecture, you can find various arts and crafts, including Yamanaka lacquerware with a history of 400 years, Kutani pottery known for its diverse expressions, and Buddhist statues that have a deep connection to Japanese history. meta.title: Yamanaka Onsen Retreat ｜ LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE YAMANAKA ONSEN RETREAT Foodways and Craftways by Mokkei & Hanamurasaki Each retreat welcomes just 4–8 guests for deep cultural immersion and exchange, as well as relaxation. You’ll stay at Hanamurasaki for three nights, with daytime explorations rooted in the open air garden kitchen of a 100-year-old kominka (folk house) at Mokkei, and branching out around Yamanaka. Prepare and share meals together with local experts, using ingredients gathered and grown nearby. Visit the workshops of a craftsperson skilled in Yamanaka’s unique lacquerware. And immerse yourself in the wild and lush natural landscape. These moments invite you to reconnect with the land, with others, and with yourself. This isn't a trip where you check things off a list, but rather a gentle opportunity to rediscover the textures of daily life. Rooted in the richness of Japan’s regional culture, this retreat proposes a new kind of travel that nurtures body and spirit through depth, not extravagance. Yamanaka Onsen is a hot spring town with a history spanning 1300 years. Nestled in the mountains along a river, nature and town coexist harmoniously. A rich food culture and traditional crafts are lovingly preserved and continue to evolve. The region is known for Yamanaka lacquerware and a cuisine shaped by the seasons, featuring locally-sourced seafood and mountain vegetables in meals that restore you from the inside out. Host Hannah Kirshner Hannah Kirshner is the author and illustrator of a book about Yamanaka’s material culture, Water, Wood, and Wild Things . She’s spent years working alongside craftspeople, documenting how their work weaves into the local culture and community. Now she connects visitors to craftways and foodways through her kitchen studio, Mokkei, in a 100-year-old farmhouse surrounded by fields and forest. Kirshner’s journalism on nature and culture appears in The New York Times and The Atlantic, and on The World radio program. Host Kohei Yamada Kohei Yamada is the sixth-generation owner of Hanamurasaki. Born and raised in Yamanaka Onsen, he has deep affection and pride for the region, along with a broad understanding of its history and culture. With a background in art and photography, he’s leading a renovation project and collaborations with regional artists. His approach to hospitality blends a modern perspective with strong respect for local crafts and nature, inviting guests to discover the enduring charm of Yamanaka Onsen. Venue Mornings will be spent at Mokkei, a kitchen studio in an old farmhouse, around a big rustic table in the open-air garden kitchen. We’re sheltered from rain by a tile roof, but can feel the season and weather changing around us. Mokkei is both community space for our local friends and neighbors and an entry point for visitors to explore Yamanaka’s rich crafts and foodways. Deeply Engage with Local Culture Rural Japan can seem impenetrable and inscrutable to visitors, but Kohei and Hannah share their deep connections with the community, bringing you into the fold. They peel back layers and translate both language and culture, welcoming you to delve into Yamanaka’s deepest culture to interact with local people. Hands-On Cooking at Mokkei Studio Gather around the big table in Mokkei’s open-air garden kitchen to learn from local chefs and home cooks, and share meals that you prepare together. These hands-on workshops offer something for professional and amateur cooks alike. You’ll bring home not just memories, but inspiration for your own table. Meaningful Connections This experience goes beyond a typical tour, it’s a cultural exchange. Share meals and conversations with Hannah and Kohei, a range of local experts, and a group of like-minded guests. You may forge connections that last a lifetime. Luxury Stay at Hanamurasaki Experience the elegance of Japanese architecture in our traditional-style rooms, or indulge in a suite featuring a private open-air bath and sauna. Seasonal cuisine, thoughtfully curated art, and heartfelt hospitality come together to create a serene and personalized retreat just for you. Next tour schedule April 26–29 reservations : Spring Retreat 2026 Completed *please check back for future retreats, or enquire about personalized itineraries Capacity: 4–8 guests Price: ¥850,000 (double) / ¥922,000 (single) *per person Day1 Day2 Day3 Day4 After checking in, come to the sabo for a cup of tea blended by Kohei with wild plants harvested by Hannah. The first day of our retreat focuses on transformation—through fermentation, cooking processes, and the deep calm you experience at Hanamurasaki. Come to Hanamurasaki’s library to learn about koji, the beneficial mold that is essential to the fermentation of sake, miso, and soy sauce. Fumiaki Matsuura, the owner and head brewer of the local sake, Shishi-no-sato sake, shares his reverence for the power of koji, and explains how he makes and uses koji rice. The conversation about koji and fermentation continues over dinner with Hanamurasaki chef, Masakazu Nakamura. Get to know your hosts and fellow guests as you share a meal that highlights fermented flavors of the region through ingredients like salt made with Matsuura’s sake, irizake (a traditional Edo-period seasoning), and ishiru (fish sauce from the Noto peninsula). Choose Japanese or Western style. Walk with Kohei along Kakusenkei gorge from Hanamurasaki to Mokkei, enjoying the scenery of the icy blue river and deep green moss and ferns. In the field around Mokkei, harvest sansai (wild vegetables and herbs.) Gather around the big table in the outdoor kitchen at Mokkei for a deep dive into dashi, the foundation of Japanese flavor, with chef Naoya Yamazaki from Engawa sake bar. Key ingredients of this essential broth were introduced across Japan by kitamaebune, merchant ships whose sailors also influenced Yamanaka’s rich folk music. Lunch in the outdoor kitchen features sansai you harvested, dashi you cooked, pickles made at Mokkei throughout the year, and dishes prepared by chef Naoya Yamazaki—all served on tableware from local artisans. Split into two groups. Group A visits the studio of a Yamanaka lacquerware artisan, while group B has free time to relax at Hanamurasaki or explore the town (the next day the groups will switch.) Besides onsen, Yamanaka is famous for its lacquerware, Yamanaka shikki. See behind-the-scenes how this work is made and reimagined by contemporary artisans. Enjoy a quiet dinner alone or with your travel companion. Everything from the ingredients to the tableware is an expression of the season and local specialties, thoughtfully composed by Kohei and chef Masakazu Nakamura. Choose Japanese or Western style. Wagashi, Japanese sweets, vary by region and by season. Asami Kuroda, a confectioner at the Yamanaka sweets shop Sankaido, explains the seasonality and symbolism of wagashi, then guides us through shaping namagashi, fresh sweet-bean confections associated with tea ceremony. Hannah takes you to the home of her neighbor and tea teacher Toshi Oshita for a tea ceremony experience. Experience how Japanese values and aesthetics are entwined with this highly-choreographed exchange between host and guest. Local hunter Sakura Yoshida joins us to cook game meat and talk about how her work is connected to the ecology of Yamanaka. Share shishi-nabe (wild boar hot pot) cooked at the table, and served with Hannah’s homemade yuzu-kosho. Today group B visits the studio of a Yamanaka lacquerware artisan, while group A has free time to relax at Hanamurasaki or explore the town (the next day the groups will switch.) Gather for a final meal all together at this friendly gastro-pub style bar. The sake-obsessive owner, Yusuke Shimoki, shares his knowledge with warmth and passion—and you may notice echoes of tea ceremony. Sake and non-alcohol pairings are included. Choose Japanese or Western style. Leave your luggage at the front desk, then join Kohei for a final tea and farewell. Explore Yamanaka on your own with personalized recommendations, or allow us to book one of the following activities for you. ・Rickshaw Tour (no additional cost, may be cancelled in case of rain) Ride around Yamanaka in a vintage rickshaw, recently brought back to life by Yoshi Omi, a rickshaw driver from Kyoto, after decades as a museum piece. Hear Yamanaka’s fascinating stories and lore as Omi takes you to historic and scenic spots. ・Kintsugi Workshop (transportation fee not included) Experience a private session with Master Shigeki Yagi, a renowned artist who collaborated with FIAT and repairs world-class antiques. Learn the delicate art of gold-repair directly from the master and take home your own restored piece. ・Nature Hike in Ozuchi Village (transportation fee not included) Explore the nostalgic landscapes of a tiny village, guided by its only resident. designated as a. Guided by a local, you’ll discover wild mountain treasures and crystal-clear rivers hidden deep within Yamanaka’s forests, National Important Preservation District. MORE What’s Included: ・Transportation to and from Kaga Onsen Station ・Luxury accommodation at a 6th-generation family-run ryokan ・Access to onsen (hot spring baths) ・3 dinners, 3 breakfasts, and 2 lunches featuring the best local and seasonal ingredients ・Tea ceremony ・4–5 immersive experiences with local artisans and experts ・Hands-on foraging and cooking ・Interpretation by hosts and translators with specialized knowledge ・Personalized advice and reservations for local spots during free time Accommodation & Pricing Notes: ・early bird (20% save) : ¥680,000 pp (double occupancy) / ¥752,000 pp (single occupancy) ・reg. price: ¥850,000 pp (double occupancy) / ¥922,000 pp (single occupancy) ・the base price is for an authentic japanese room with futon. ・upgrades to a suite with a private open-air onsen bath are available upon request. Please refer to the room types and contact us for availability. Please come prepared to be outdoors for half the day. The space at Mokkei where our workshops and lunches take place is an open air kitchen. We have a roof over our heads, but just screens for walls, so we feel the changing seasons and weather. The exact schedule and activities may change due to weather, illness, or other elements beyond our control, but we will make reasonable (and delightful) substitutions. Next tour schedule October 30 – November 3, 2026 reservations : Please inquire individually Capacity: Please inquire individually Price: Please inquire individually Day1 Day2 Day3 Day4 MORE What’s Included: ・Transportation to and from Kaga Onsen Station ・Luxury accommodation at a 6th-generation family-run ryokan ・Access to onsen (hot spring baths) ・3 dinners, 3 breakfasts, and 2 lunches featuring the best local and seasonal ingredients ・Tea ceremony ・4–5 immersive experiences with local artisans and experts ・Hands-on foraging and cooking ・Interpretation by hosts and translators with specialized knowledge ・Personalized advice and reservations for local spots during free time Accommodation & Pricing Notes: ・the base price is for an authentic japanese room with futon. ・upgrades to a suite with a private open-air onsen bath are available upon request. Please refer to the room types and contact us for availability. Please come prepared to be outdoors for half the day. The space at Mokkei where our workshops and lunches take place is an open air kitchen. We have a roof over our heads, but just screens for walls, so we feel the changing seasons and weather. The exact schedule and activities may change due to weather, illness, or other elements beyond our control, but we will make reasonable (and delightful) substitutions. If the scheduled dates don’t suit you, we’d be delighted to discus a tour that works with your plans. Please enquire if you’re interested in a private retreat or tour, tailored to the interests of your own small group. meta.title: Sake & Fermentation ｜ LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE TOUR PAGE Discovering the World of Sake and Fermentation Across the Counter Time : About 1.5 hours (Open from 14:00) Price : ¥5,000 per person Capacity : Up to 5 people This culinary experience invites you to enjoy both Japanese sake and fermented foods at 和酒BAR縁がわ (Washu Bar Engawa), hosted by Yusuke Shimoki, the first person in Ishikawa Prefecture to hold the prestigious Sakasho (Master of Sake) certification. Deeply connected to local sake breweries and the region’s traditional craft of Yamanaka lacquerware, Shimoki guides guests through the depth of sake and the character of the land through quiet conversation across the counter. By savoring the pairing of sake and fermented dishes while listening to the stories of their makers, participants are invited to experience the essence of Japanese food culture. ◼︎ Sake Talk by Yusuke Shimoki From the perspective of a certified sake master, Shimoki introduces the appeal of Japanese sake, its relationship with vessels, and the beauty of serving gestures, accompanied by live demonstrations. Guests experience sake not only through taste, but also through movement and presence. ◼︎ Sake Tasting A tasting of three carefully selected local sakes, chosen to complement food. Guests compare aromas, flavors, and the unique character created through fermentation. ◼︎ Fermented Dishes Fermented snacks prepared by Japanese chef Naoya Yamazaki , rooted in Ishikawa’s fermentation culture. Each dish is thoughtfully paired to enhance the flavors of the sake. Born from the need to survive harsh winters, Ishikawa’s fermentation culture tells a story of resilience. Through this experience, guests are invited to explore its history and origins. Next to the bar is Hasebe Shrine, a small shrine dedicated to the local hot spring. Lush moss and a gently flowing stream within the grounds create a calming atmosphere. We invite you to enjoy a quiet stroll. meta.title: Pottery Painting Tour ｜ LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE TOUR PAGE Exclusive Pottery Painting at a Private Kiln with Matcha Ceremony Time : About 2 hours Price : ¥20,000 per person (+¥10,000 for interpretation) Capacity : Up to 5 people This exclusive program invites you to visit a renowned pottery artist, located just a 3-minute stroll from the ryokan, where you’ll experience the art of pottery painting. This kiln is generally not open to the public, making this a special tour exclusively for guests staying at Hanamurasaki. Maikeigama kiln specializes in producing tea ceremony utensils and is currently run by the fourth-generation head, Eiju Yaguchi. Usually, they work exclusively on orders from tea masters, with general sales limited to local visits or exhibitions. The kiln, which is over 100 years old, and the adjoining tea room are protected structures that cannot be built under current laws, preserving traditional construction techniques. This experience allows you to paint a sake cup from the “Birds Resting” series, a piece that has been passed down through generations, followed by an opportunity to enjoy matcha. The pottery pieces you painted will be finished by the artist and ready for pickup by your checkout the next day. meta.title: Kintsugi Tour ｜ LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE TOUR PAGE Kintsugi Private Workshop Time : About 2 hours Price : ¥25,000 per person (transport extra , 20 minutes by taxi) Capacity : Up to 4 people This specialized Kintsugi workshop receives repair requests from all over the country. Shigeki Yagi, the Kintsugi master, handles repairs not only for Japanese antiques but also for items like old Baccarat glass. He has also presented as a Kintsugi artist at FIAT's 120th anniversary event, "FIAT x MADE IN JAPAN PROJECT." This workshop offers a special tour where participants can learn Kintsugi directly from Yagi himself, and you can take home your repaired piece. You can take home the kintsugi pieces you created after the experience. Kintsugi turns broken pottery into beautiful art, celebrating its unique story. meta.title: Private Ikebana Tour ｜ LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE TOUR PAGE Private Ikebana Workshop Time : About 3 hours Price : ¥25,000 per person ( +¥10,000 for interpretation ) Capacity : 2 to 3 people ( for guests staying 2 nights or more ) This special workshop is led by Mr. Nakamura, an ikebana artist trained in the Koryu school, a traditional form of ikebana that dates back to the 1760s. Program Details: 1. Ikebana Demonstration Enjoy a demonstration by Mr. Nakamura as he creates a beautiful ikebana arrangement. 2. Fieldwork in Nature Explore Japan’s lush woodlands and gather plants ideal for ikebana (Fieldwork is subject to cancellation in case of rain). 3. Ikebana Creation Experience Under Mr. Nakamura’s guidance, create your own original ikebana arrangement using the plants you have collected. 4. In-Room Display Your ikebana arrangement will be displayed in your guest room, allowing you to enjoy the harmony between your creation and the space during your stay (Flower containers are not available for take-home). Location: At Hanamurasaki Reservation: Please book at least one month in advance. meta.title: Hiking Tour ｜ LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE TOUR PAGE Hiking in Satoyama Nature at Yamanaka Onsen Time : About 2 hours Price : ¥10,000 per person (transport extra, 30 min by taxi) Capacity : Up to 4 people Take a hike to the most nature-rich area of Yamanaka Onsen and explore the tiny, lovery village of Ōzuchi, home to just one resident. Designated as an Important Preservation District for Groups of Traditional Buildings, this area preserves the nostalgic landscapes of old Japan. Discover wild plants and mushrooms underfoot, admire the beautiful forests above, and enjoy the crystal-clear upstream river. This special tour takes you around the entire village, guided by the local resident, Mr. Nobo. Enjoy meaningful conversations with the local people. meta.title: Private Cycling Tour ｜ LOCAL EXPERIENCE ｜ Japan Ishikawa Pref. Japanese inn (Ryokan) Onsen Hanamurasaki

sections.body: LOCAL EXPERIENCE TOUR PAGE Private Cycling Tour: Where Nature and Craftsmanship Meet Time : Duration: 4–8 hours (depending on course) Price : From ¥50,000 (per group + individual fee) Capacity : Up to 4 people Yamanaka Onsen is a peaceful town where rich nature and local life blend harmoniously.This cycling tour offers a unique chance to meet local artisans and explore hidden natural spots accessible only with a guide.Start your ride in the fresh morning air on an electric-assist E-Bike, led by a knowledgeable local guide.Choose from three plans to suit your time and energy, and discover the deep charm of Yamanaka. ¥35,000 per group + ¥15,000 per person Time: 9:00 AM – 1:00 PM Departing from the ryokan, this route takes you through the serene forests of Yamanaka. You’ll visit a historic dam park with a beautiful bridge, learn about the origins of Kutani porcelain at old kiln sites, and stop by a local craftsman's workshop to experience the region’s living traditions. ¥35,000 per group + ¥18,000 per person Time: 9:00 AM – 3:00 PM Includes everything in the Standard Tour, plus a relaxing lunch break at a locally loved café or farm restaurant. A half-day retreat that lets you savor both the flavors and natural beauty of the area. ¥35,000 per group + ¥21,000 per person Time: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM For those who want to fully immerse themselves in the culture and landscape of Yamanaka. In addition to the Lunch Tour, this route includes visits to Takigahara — a village known for its stone heritage — and the historic Natadera Temple.An unforgettable day of deep exploration through nature, history, and craftsmanship. ・Please wear comfortable clothing and shoes suitable for cycling. ・Routes may be adjusted depending on weather and road conditions.
